title: StructText: A Synthetic Table-to-Text Approach for Benchmark Generation with Multi-Dimensional Evaluation
authors: Satyananda Kashyap, Sola Shirai, Nandana Mihindukulasooriya, Horst Samulowitz (all IBM Research)
venue: VLDB 2025 Workshop: Tabular Data Analysis (TaDA); arXiv:2507.21340, v1 only, submitted 28 Jul 2025
claim: StructText is an automated, two-stage 'plan-then-execute' pipeline that turns existing tabular data into synthetic natural-language text, then uses that (text, ground-truth table) pair as a contamination-resistant benchmark source for text-to-table extraction. The paper's central empirical claim is a dichotomy: LLMs generating the synthetic text achieve near-ceiling factuality, hallucination-avoidance, and numeric/temporal fidelity, but the resulting narrative prose has low coherence and is difficult for an LLM-based baseline extractor to parse back into structured key-value pairs (Abstract; Sec. 7.1).
novelty: The authors present three claimed contributions versus prior structured-to-text/key-value benchmarks (ToTTo, LogicNLG, WebNLG, WikiBio, KVP10K, CORD): (1) an automatic, human-annotation-free pipeline for generating text-to-table benchmarks from arbitrary tabular data via an LLM 'plan-then-execute' report-generation workflow (Sec. 1.1, Sec. 3.1); (2) a multi-dimensional evaluation framework combining LLM-as-judge rubrics (factuality, hallucination, coherence) with objective numeric/temporal extraction metrics, which they say prior benchmarks lack (Sec. 1.1, Sec. 2, Table 1); (3) a large-scale benchmark (71,539 examples / 49-50 datasets in different passages) spanning SEC financial filings and WikiDB tables reflecting 'real-world complexity' versus prior narrow single-domain datasets, plus a released baseline text-to-table extraction system and full open-source artifacts (Sec. 1.1, Sec. 5.1, HuggingFace/GitHub links in header).

protocol.description: This paper is not itself a canonicalization/exact-match benchmark; it is a benchmark-generation and extraction-baseline paper with two separate LLM call types, both single forward passes (no interactive/multi-turn correction loop is described): (a) Report generation — the model receives ~10 sample table rows plus a planning prompt, must autonomously group columns into 1-5 'report types' (Sec. 3.1, Planning Stage), then in a second single-shot call receives the report-type name and selected columns' key-value pairs (e.g., 'revenue: 2.65595E+11; revenue_period: 9/29/18; ...') and must produce one coherent natural-language paragraph per row strictly using only those columns (Sec. 3.1, Generation Stage; Fig. 2 shows the literal key-value string given to the model and the prose it returns). (b) Baseline extraction — given report text, the model first predicts a plausible column schema from a sample of reports (one call), then for each row is fed the report text plus predicted columns and must return 'a JSON-formatted dictionary of key-value pairs corresponding to column-value extractions' (Sec. 4.1) — again a single-shot structured-output call (DSPy-enforced), not an iterative repair loop. Demonstrations/few-shot exemplars are not described for either step; temperature is fixed to 0 for determinism (Sec. 5.2). No paper-native verbatim prompt template is quoted beyond the key-value string and generated-paragraph pairs shown in Fig. 2 and the worked example in Fig. 5.
protocol.observation_content: For generation: the model sees a semicolon-delimited flat key-value string of the selected columns for one row (see Fig. 2 example: 'revenue: 2.65595E+11; revenue_period: 9/29/18; ...; stock_ticker: AAPL; company_name: Apple Inc. ...') and must output a full natural-language paragraph reproducing those facts; no partial/turn-by-turn feedback is given. For extraction: the model sees one synthetic report's full prose text plus a list of target column names, and returns a single JSON dict of extracted key-value pairs in one pass (Sec. 4.1) — there is no described mechanism for the model to see per-field correctness feedback and retry within the same instance.

representations: The paper does not run a controlled ablation of input/output representation formats for a fixed task (e.g., JSON vs. flattened string vs. natural language) the way a canonicalization benchmark would. The only representation contrast reported is across pipeline stages: (1) source tabular data (rows/columns with numeric, date, and categorical cells), (2) an intermediate flat key-value string fed to the generation LLM (Fig. 2), (3) free natural-language prose output by the generation LLM, and (4) JSON-dict output by the extraction LLM (Sec. 4.1). The paper's core finding is precisely that this representation shift (structured -> prose -> structured) is where fidelity is lost: numeric/temporal facts survive the structured-to-prose step with high fidelity (Table 3, all F1 > 0.9 except SEC numeric F1 0.927) but survive the prose-to-structured (extraction) step poorly (Table 4, F1 as low as 0.413/0.455 for column ID and value extraction unscored due to low precision/recall ~0.11-0.26). No comparison of alternative canonical serialization formats (e.g., differing key orders, quoting, whitespace conventions) is reported anywhere in the paper.

models.name: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct
models.strategies: Used only for report planning and text generation (structured-to-text direction); single-shot prompting per stage (plan-then-execute), temperature 0, DSPy framework enforcing structured intermediate outputs (Sec. 5.2). No CoT, few-shot, or ReAct strategy is described.
models.key_results: Drives the near-ceiling factuality/hallucination scores in Table 2 (SEC: factuality 4.58, hallucination 4.90, coherence 3.28, overall 4.25; WikiDB: factuality 4.56, hallucination 4.55, coherence 3.53, overall 4.21) and the numeric/temporal fidelity in Table 3 (Numeric SEC P/R/F1 = 0.941/0.924/0.927; Numeric WikiDB = 0.849/0.971/0.956; Temporal SEC = 0.818/0.977/0.956; Temporal WikiDB = 0.878/0.916/0.915).

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct
models.strategies: Used as 'the primary LLM for all the evaluation components' (LLM-as-judge scoring) and implicitly as the baseline text-to-table extraction model in Sec. 4 (paper does not explicitly re-state which model performs extraction, but Sec. 5.2 says Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct was used for 'the evaluation part' broadly). Temperature 0, DSPy structured outputs, single-shot schema-prediction then single-shot value-extraction (Sec. 4.1).
models.key_results: Drives the baseline extraction numbers in Table 4: Column Identification SEC P/R/F1 = 0.344/0.669/0.455; Column Identification WikiDB = 0.395/0.433/0.413; Value Extraction SEC P/R = 0.257/0.11 (F1 not reported, marked '-'); Value Extraction WikiDB P/R = 0.179/0.137 (F1 not reported).

headline_numbers: Table 2 (LLM-as-judge, 1-5 scale): SEC Financial factuality 4.58, hallucination 4.90, coherence 3.28, overall 4.25; WikiDB Tables factuality 4.56, hallucination 4.55, coherence 3.53, overall 4.21 — coherence is the clear weak dimension in both domains (Sec. 6.1).

Table 3 (numeric/temporal accuracy): all four Precision/Recall/F1 triples exceed 0.9 on F1 except SEC numeric precision 0.941/recall 0.924/F1 0.927 (still >0.9); WikiDB numeric P 0.849 with F1 0.956 despite lower precision (Sec. 6.2).

Table 4 (baseline text-to-table extraction): Column Identification F1 only 0.455 (SEC) / 0.413 (WikiDB); Value Extraction precision/recall as low as 0.11-0.26, with F1 not computed/reported for value extraction (Sec. 6.3) — this is the paper's headroom/floor evidence that narrative text resists automated re-structuring even when facts are accurately stated.

Quality filtering: sweeping temporal-precision threshold tau from 1.0 down to 0.70 in 0.05 steps retained over 50% of SEC reports even at tau = 0.90 while improving overall metrics (Sec. 3.3, Sec. 6.4, Fig. 4).

Worked example (Fig. 5, Hilton Worldwide): temporal parsing precision 80% (4/5), recall 100% (4/4), F1 88.89%, illustrating a benign granularity-mismatch failure ('fiscal year 2024' correctly extracted as a temporal value but absent from ground-truth columns).

Scale: 71,539 examples across 49 datasets overall (Abstract), with the reported experiments run on a representative subset of 50 datasets (SEC filings + 1,000-table WikiDB subset averaging 11.6 columns/152.8 rows) (Sec. 5.1, Sec. 6).
failure_modes: The paper's single reported failure mode class is a structured-to-prose-to-structured information-accessibility gap: LLMs preserve individual facts (numbers, dates) with high fidelity when narrativizing tables (Table 3, all near/above 0.9) but the resulting prose has low coherence (Table 2, coherence ~3.3-3.5 vs. ~4.5-4.9 for factuality/hallucination) and its facts become difficult to re-extract as structured key-value pairs (Table 4, extraction F1 as low as 0.41-0.46 for columns and near-floor precision/recall of 0.11-0.26 for values) (Sec. 6.1-6.3, Sec. 7.1). A secondary, more granular failure mode is measurement/parsing ambiguity rather than a model failure per se: the worked Hilton example shows the temporal parser correctly extracting 'fiscal year 2024' as a valid date-like value that was never present as a discrete ground-truth column, deflating precision even though 'neither the text generation nor quality evaluation were technically incorrect' (Sec. 6.4, Fig. 5 caption). The paper also notes a domain-specific asymmetry: SEC data yields high extraction recall but very low precision (over-inclusion of information), while WikiDB extraction is more balanced/precise (Sec. 6.3).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper does not test or discuss an interactive/multi-turn correction loop at all — both generation and extraction are described and run as single forward-pass LLM calls per stage (plan then generate; predict schema then extract values), with no feedback-and-retry mechanism reported (Sec. 3.1, Sec. 4.1, Sec. 5.2). Its conclusion is framed purely in terms of what a single generation pass can and cannot preserve: models can, in one pass, both plan meaningful column groupings (Fig. 1) and generate factually faithful, numerically/temporally precise narrative text (Table 2, Table 3), but that same one-pass narrative is not itself amenable to being parsed back into structured form by a downstream one-pass extractor (Table 4) — the paper frames this as a property of the text produced, not as evidence about whether more demonstrations or iterative refinement would close the extraction gap (that possibility is left to 'future work'/'community' extension, Sec. 7.3), so it offers no direct evidence either way about few-shot/many-shot or multi-turn effects on either generation or extraction accuracy.

relation_to_candidate.position: This paper sits far from c11 on the family's key protocol axes. It is single-shot per call (no interactive loop) on both halves of its pipeline, matching c11's single-shot mode, but everything else differs: what is observed is a full row of heterogeneous table cells (numbers, dates, categorical strings) rather than c11's single messy string/record under invented rules; the model must produce free natural-language narrative text (generation direction) or a best-effort JSON dict with model-chosen keys (extraction direction) rather than one exact canonical string reproducing a fixed reference function; and the verifier is a graded, multi-dimensional bundle (5-point LLM-as-judge rubrics for factuality/hallucination/coherence, plus fuzzy/edit-distance-and-bipartite-matched precision/recall/F1 for extraction, Sec. 4.2) rather than c11's strict 0/1 whole-string exact match against a deterministic ~40-line reference canonicalizer.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: The overlap is narrow and structural rather than task-level: both projects start from a tabular/record data source and use a table -> derived-artifact -> reference-comparison pipeline to build a synthetic, regenerable, contamination-resistant instance pool (StructText Sec. 1.1/3.1; c11's 'infinite, contamination-proof pool' framing). Both also emphasize that ground truth is fully specified by construction (the source table row for StructText; the ~40-line reference canonicalizer for c11), enabling automatic, human-annotation-free scoring at scale.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Direction: StructText's core generative direction is table-to-text (structured to prose); c11 is the reverse and more constrained: mostly-normalization/serialization of already-given values into one fixed string, not free text generation. (2) Verifier strictness: StructText explicitly uses tolerant fuzzy metrics (Levenshtein similarity, BERTScore, 0.1% numeric relative-error tolerance, bipartite column matching) precisely because exact string match is not the target; c11 is the opposite extreme, whole-string exact match with zero partial credit. (3) Rule invention: StructText's 'house conventions' are the LLM's own emergent column groupings and narrative choices (nothing is imposed by a fixed rule table); c11's house rules are pre-specified, adversarial-by-construction deviations from a named public standard (RFC 8785 JCS) that the model must have inferred/memorized or extract from limited demonstrations. (4) Failure locus: StructText's headline finding is that facts survive generation but not re-extraction; c11 has no generation step at all — its harness only ever tests the canonicalization/normalization direction.
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add a deterministic, single-string, zero-partial-credit scoring regime entirely absent from StructText's multi-dimensional/fuzzy evaluation design; a rule-level diagnostic capability (StructText's extraction metrics are aggregate precision/recall over an entire report's fields, whereas c11's stratified rule design lets each wrong house rule show up as a distinct, attributable failure in the expected/got diff); and a focus on inferring/applying explicitly adversarial, standard-deviating conventions rather than StructText's focus on whether narrative fluency and information-accessibility trade off against each other during free generation.

corpus_claim: StructText (Kashyap et al., 2025) is a synthetic table-to-text generator reversible into a canonicalization instance source; cited for c12 but applies to c11's synthetic-pool design.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper does support the core mechanical claim that it is a synthetic, automated table-to-text generator built from real tabular data (SEC filings, WikiDB), producing (text, ground-truth-table) pairs at scale (71,539 examples / 49 datasets; Sec. 1.1, Sec. 5.1) — this part of the corpus claim is accurate. However, the paper never proposes, discusses, or evaluates a 'canonicalization' task or reversibility into a canonicalizer-style benchmark: its entire evaluation apparatus (5-point LLM-as-judge rubrics, fuzzy Levenshtein/BERTScore column matching, 0.1% numeric tolerance) is designed around fuzzy, tolerant scoring of free-text generation and text-to-table extraction, not exact-match reproduction of a deterministic house-style serializer. The word 'canonicalization'/'canonical' does not appear in the paper's framing of its own contribution (it appears only incidentally as 'normalised ground-truth numeric values' in the numeric-parsing methodology, Sec. 3.2). So the 'reversible into a canonicalization instance source' framing is an inference/extrapolation by the candidate dossier author, not a claim the paper itself makes — it is a plausible re-purposing (StructText's row-to-string generation direction could in principle be inverted/adapted to seed a normalization task), but StructText as written is not a canonicalization benchmark or generator, and its exact-match / house-rule-deviation ideas (central to c11) are entirely absent from the paper.
baseline_relevant: Scale/difficulty dials actually used: 10 sample rows shown to the LLM for planning (Sec. 3.1); 1-5 report types per table (hard cap); WikiDB subset averaged 11.6 columns and 152.8 rows per table (Sec. 5.1); over 40 columns per SEC company (Sec. 5.1).

Temperature fixed to 0 for determinism across all model calls, with DSPy used to enforce structured output parsing (Sec. 5.2) — directly relevant to designing a deterministic, reproducible un-optimized baseline harness.

Metrics/verifier design used: for generation quality, 5-point LLM-as-judge rubrics (factuality, hallucination, coherence) plus regex+NER-based numeric parsing (0.1% relative error tolerance) and LLM+SUTime-based temporal parsing; for extraction quality, precision/recall/F1 with Levenshtein-distance/BERTScore column-name similarity and OR-tools bipartite matching between predicted and ground-truth columns (Sec. 3.2, Sec. 4.2) — none of this is exact-match, unlike what c11 needs, but it is a template for how a tolerant secondary metric could be built if c11 ever wanted partial-credit diagnostics.

Observed floors/ceilings: generation-side scores are near-ceiling (factuality/hallucination 4.5-4.9/5, numeric/temporal F1 >0.9), while extraction-side scores are far below ceiling (column ID F1 0.41-0.46; value extraction precision/recall 0.11-0.26) — evidence that a downstream exact/structured-recovery task built on LLM-generated free text is a much harder floor than generation itself, a useful prior for calibrating c11's difficulty dials if any generation step is ever added to its pipeline.

Quality filtering as a difficulty/contamination control: sweeping a threshold tau (1.0 to 0.70 in 0.05 steps) on the weakest metric (temporal precision for SEC) retained >50% of the dataset even at tau=0.90 (Sec. 3.3, Sec. 6.4) — a possible analogue for how c11 might calibrate its own rule-stratum difficulty or drop overly hard strata without collapsing pool size.

Release/contamination status: full framework, datasets, evaluation tools, and baseline extraction code released at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ibm-research/struct-text and https://github.com/ibm/struct-text (header, Abstract 'VLDB Workshop Artifact Availability'); SEC data is regenerable on demand from the public SEC EDGAR XBRL API (Sec. 5.1), which the authors argue avoids 'data distribution concerns' — directly analogous to c11's own contamination-proofing-via-regeneration design goal.

Model choice caveat: the paper used only one model per role (Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct for generation, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct for evaluation/extraction) and explicitly flags this as a scope limitation, deferring 'a spectrum of model sizes and architectures' to future work (Sec. 7.3) — relevant caution for not over-generalizing single-model baseline numbers when designing c11's own baseline runs.
quotes: "We evaluated the proposed method on 71,539 examples across 49 datasets. Results reveal that while LLMs achieve strong factual accuracy and avoid hallucination, they struggle with narrative coherence in producing extractable text." (Abstract)

"Our LLM based system first analyzes ten sample rows from the input table and must autonomously identify data patterns and relationships to produce meaningful report structures... We limit the output to one to five report types." (Sec. 3.1, Planning Stage)

"For each row, we feed in the report text together with the predicted set of columns to extract, and produce a JSON-formatted dictionary of key-value pairs corresponding to column-value extractions." (Sec. 4.1)

"The results demonstrate exceptionally strong performance in factual accuracy with scores of 4.58 & 4.56 for factuality and 4.90 & 4.55 for hallucination avoidance... However, coherence scores reveals a notable performance gap." (Sec. 6.1)

"The results demonstrate strong performances with both numeric extractions and temporal extraction achieving a score greater than 0.9... The strong performance contrasts to the key-value extraction highlighting that while specific facts are preserved accurately, the semantic relations and attributions become obscured in narrative form." (Sec. 6.2)
"The results revealed significant headroom in performance in automated information extraction from generated text, demonstrating that the benchmark is sufficiently challenging for LLMs." (Sec. 6.3)
"This simple strategy kept over 50% of SEC reports when τ = 0.90 while also improving the overall metrics." (Sec. 3.3)

"Our quantitative study focused on a single strong open-weights model (Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct model for report generation and Meta's Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct for evaluation) so that we could prototype the end-to-end evaluation pipeline... In future work, we plan to benchmark a spectrum of model sizes and architectures." (Sec. 7.3)
